smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: 3 ads for Barrelhand
thread.body_text:
hey Karel, spent a couple minutes on the Barrelhand site and honestly can't figure out why we're not already working together haha.

anyway, that's not why I'm writing. I run Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of pitching you some BS, I actually built you a few. (gotta provide value before anything)

built 3 ad mockups from Barrelhand's real products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and reviews. it's done and sitting ready on my end.

want me to send it over?

- Kyle

Hi Kyle, 

 We are currently running meta ads, would be curious what you had in mind and how you would approach it differently.
 

 -Karel

here you go Karel - 3 ad mockups built from Barrelhand's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Barrelhand sells a $9,750 space-grade mechanical watch called Monolith, has no visible reviews and no ads we can find, so this call is about proving one angle can work at a high price, not scaling fast, and Karel already said they run Meta ads so ask what is actually happening before pitching anything.

deep_dive.what_they_are: An engineering-first independent watch brand out of San Francisco. They frame themselves as building tools for space exploration, not a heritage luxury house. The site is more mission and manifesto than shop.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Their flagship is the Monolith, an ultra-premium mechanical toolwatch priced at $9,750. It comes in three strap colorways: Barrelhand Orange, Black, and Black & Orange. Next batch deliveries start Q4 2026, and orders are fully refundable before shipment. They also have a Memory Disc project and a 'Project One'. Do not guess at what those cost or contain, we do not have that data.
deep_dive.positioning: Top-tier, technical, mission-driven. Frontier engineering for serious collectors, not fashion-luxury. 'A window to the future.' 'Spaceship for your wrist.'
deep_dive.who_buys: Serious watch collectors and space and technology enthusiasts with high disposable income. People who value provable engineering, being early to a frontier story, and products they can service and adapt over time.
strengths: Very clear and unusual brand story. 'Spaceship for your wrist' and 'window to the future' are strong hooks that ads can lean on.
High price means one sale is worth a lot, so even a small number of buyers can justify real ad spend.
gaps: No visible reviews or social proof anywhere on the site. At $9,750 that is a real trust problem for cold traffic.
We could not find any Meta ads running, yet Karel says they run them. So either they are small, paused, or we just cannot see them. We do not actually know what they are doing today.

game_plan.headline: Prove one story-led angle can turn a stranger into a $9,750 buyer, then put more money behind the winner and widen the audience.

phases.goal: Prove that paid traffic can produce real interest at this price. At $9,750, most people will not buy on the first click, so early success is measured in qualified leads and newsletter signups, not a pile of instant sales.
campaigns: One testing campaign with the three ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet, each as its own ad set so we can see which angle people respond to.
A simple retargeting campaign that follows up with anyone who watched the video or visited the Monolith page.
lead_products: Monolith. It is the only product we have a confirmed price and clear story for, and the whole site points to it. We lead with it because it is the hero, not a guess.
phases.creative: Start with the strongest hook from the sheet, the 'Spaceship for your wrist' angle, because it turns a watch into a story in four words and matches how their own buyers talk. Run the other two ads alongside it. The goal of month one is to learn which angle pulls, not to spend big.
phases.budget: Start small and deliberate. See the estimates below for the number and why.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One angle clearly beats the others. We see cheap, quality traffic landing on the Monolith page, people watching the video through, and a steady trickle of newsletter signups or refundable pre-orders. We are not promising sales in month one at this price.

phases.goal: Move budget onto the winning angle and widen who sees it. Build enough retargeting audience to work the long buying cycle a $9,750 watch needs.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad set and cut the losers.
Build one or two fresh versions of the winning angle so it does not go stale.
Stronger retargeting that speaks to trust: the engineering, the serviceability, the refundable-before-shipment promise.
lead_products: Monolith still leads. We do not add a second product until we know the first one can be sold with ads.
phases.creative: Take whatever won in month one and make more of it. Lean into the trust gap with content that shows the engineering and the fully-refundable-before-shipment offer, since that lowers the risk of a big purchase.
phases.budget: Increase behind the proven winner only. No blind spend increase.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The cost to get a serious lead is trending down, the retargeting audience is growing, and we can point to real pre-orders or booked sales calls, not just clicks.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and test a second product to open a lower or different price entry point.
campaigns: Scale the winning Monolith campaign further.
Test one campaign for a second offer, likely the Memory Disc or Project One, once Karel tells us the price and what it is.
Full retargeting funnel across all product interest.
lead_products: Monolith as the scaled winner. Plus a second product test. We cannot name which one to lead with yet because we do not have prices for Memory Disc or Project One. That is a discovery question.
phases.creative: Keep refreshing the Monolith winner. Build a first set of ads for the second product using the same brand voice.
phases.budget: Scale the winner, ring-fence a small test budget for the second product so it does not eat the proven campaign.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Monolith ads are running profitably at a higher spend, and we have early read on whether a second product can bring in buyers at a different price point.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get a meaningful read on which of the three ads people respond to within a few weeks. We keep it modest on purpose because at a $9,750 price the buying cycle is long, so month one is about learning, not spending.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We would need Karel's numbers before putting one here
estimates.basis: at $9,750 a unit, adding even one or two sales changes the whole picture, and we have no data on their current sales, their margin, or how long a buyer takes to decide. Guessing a revenue figure here would be dishonest. This is a discovery item, not an estimate.

assumptions: The Monolith is their hero product and the main thing worth advertising.
The $9,750 price and the fully-refundable-before-shipment offer are both still current.
They can handle a long buying cycle, meaning the business does not need instant sales to justify ad spend.
Their margin on a near-$10k watch is healthy enough to carry paid ads, which we still need Karel to confirm.

questions.question: You mentioned you're already running Meta ads. Can you walk me through what that looks like right now, roughly what you spend a month and who's running it?
questions.listening_for: Whether it is them, a freelancer, or another agency. How much is behind it. If the number is tiny or paused, this is really a from-zero build, not a rescue.

questions.question: When someone buys a Monolith at $9,750, how much of that is actually margin after the watch is built and shipped?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is enough room to pay for ads. On a near-$10k product the margin should be there, but you need him to say it, not you to assume it.

questions.question: How long does a typical buyer take from first hearing about you to actually ordering?
questions.listening_for: If it is weeks or months, that shapes everything. It means we judge month one on leads and signups, not sales, and he needs to be okay with that.

questions.question: Have you tried paid ads before this, and what happened?
questions.listening_for: Past disappointment, unrealistic expectations, or a specific thing that broke. Tells you what he is scared of on this call.

questions.question: When people land on the Monolith page from an ad, what usually happens? Do they buy, book a call, join the newsletter, or just leave?
questions.listening_for: Where the funnel is leaking. If traffic comes but nobody converts, it is a trust or offer problem, not an ad problem.

questions.question: I couldn't find any customer reviews or testimonials on the site. Do you have any, even private ones, from people who own a Monolith?
questions.listening_for: Whether any social proof exists at all. At this price, no proof is the single biggest thing working against cold ads. If he has some, we build ads around it fast.

questions.question: Right now, what's the main action you want an ad to drive: a full purchase, a refundable pre-order, or a newsletter signup?
questions.listening_for: His real expectation. If he expects instant $9,750 checkouts from cold traffic, you need to reset that gently on the call.

questions.question: Tell me about the Memory Disc and Project One. What are they, and what do they cost?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is a lower-priced entry product that could bring people in cheaper. This decides what the phase 3 second-product test looks like.

questions.question: Do you get people searching for you by name, or searching things like 'space watch' or 'aerospace toolwatch' and finding you?
questions.listening_for: If collectors are already searching for this kind of watch, Google search ads are a real opening because those people have high intent. If nobody searches for it yet, say so and skip it.

questions.question: How do most people find you today, word of mouth, watch forums, press, or something else?
questions.listening_for: If watch collector communities and press are driving them, there may be an SEO and content opening around the engineering story. If it is all word of mouth, hold off and focus on Meta first.

objections.objection: We already run Meta ads, so why would we switch to you?
objections.answer: You said that, and it's exactly why I built you three real ads before this call instead of pitching. The question isn't switching, it's whether what you're running is actually pulling the right buyer at this price. Show me what you've got running and I'll tell you straight if it's working or where it's leaking.

objections.objection: Our watch is $9,750. Nobody buys something that expensive from a Facebook ad.
objections.answer: Agreed, and I'm not going to pretend they will. At this price the ad's job in month one is to start the relationship, get the right person watching your story and joining your list, not to close a ten thousand dollar sale on the first click. We build the trust across weeks, using your refundable-before-shipment offer to lower the risk. That's why I want us to judge month one on quality leads, not instant sales.

objections.objection: We have no reviews or testimonials to point to. Won't that hurt the ads?
objections.answer: It's the one thing I'd fix first, and I flagged it honestly. At this price people need proof. If you have any owners, even a handful, we build ads around them. If you don't yet, we lean hard on the engineering, the serviceability, and the refundable offer to carry trust while we gather proof. I'd rather tell you this now than after you've spent money.

objections.objection: Isn't a small starting budget too slow for us?
objections.answer: For a near-ten-thousand-dollar product with a long buying cycle, going big before we know which angle works just burns money. We start deliberate, find the angle people respond to, then put real money behind the winner in month two. One extra Monolith sale covers a lot of testing.

red_flags.flag: No visible reviews or social proof anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: At $9,750, cold buyers need heavy proof before they trust the brand. With nothing to point to, ads have to work much harder and conversions will be slower. This must be addressed early or the plan struggles.

red_flags.flag: We could not find any Meta ads running, but Karel says they run them.
red_flags.why_it_matters: There is a gap between what we can see and what he claims. Do not assume you know their setup. Ask before you pitch, or you risk saying something wrong on the call.

red_flags.flag: Very high price with a long, considered buying cycle.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Nobody impulse-buys a $9,750 watch. Sales will be slow and lumpy. If Karel expects fast returns, expectations need resetting on the call or he will judge the work as failing when it is actually normal.

red_flags.flag: Next batch deliveries not until Q4 2026 and no scrapable catalog.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Long wait for delivery can cool buyers, and we cannot see full product or price data beyond Monolith. Do not name prices or products we were not given. Confirm everything with Karel first.

other_channel_openings: Google search ads may be worth a look if collectors already search for terms like 'space watch' or 'aerospace toolwatch', because those people have high buying intent. Confirm with the discovery question first, do not assume the demand exists.

SEO and content around the engineering and space story could pay off long term if watch forums and press are already talking about them. Only pursue if the discovery answers show real search demand, not a brand new category nobody looks for yet.
